Post_Text: Same hospital. Same surgery. Same surgeon. Your plan paid one number. Medicare pays a fraction of it. You paid the big one and nobody showed you the small one: [LINK]
A staff member had surgery. The hospital would have taken a fraction of what your plan paid. Nobody ever showed the school the smaller number. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)
Your network "discount" sounds like a win. It's a discount off a list price the hospital invented. You can negotiate hard against a fake number and still overpay: [LINK]
The chargemaster is a hospital's list price. It is set high on purpose and tied to nothing. A 50 percent discount off a made-up number is still a made-up number: [LINK]
Stop arguing about the discount. Start defining the price. That one shift is the whole idea behind reference-based pricing: [LINK]
Your network discount is calculated off a price the hospital invented. Here is what it looks like to pay off a real benchmark instead. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)

Post_Text: Hospital and facility costs are the heaviest line in your health plan. Trimming the edges of a network plan never touches it. Here is how schools cut the price basis on the line that actually matters: [LINK]

Anyone selling reference-based pricing as free money is lying to you. The real risk is balance billing, and a serious program defends against it. Read the tradeoff said out loud, not buried in a pitch deck: [LINK]

One question for whoever handles your school's benefits: On your biggest hospital claims last year, has anyone shown you what they would have cost benchmarked against Medicare, next to what your plan actually paid? If the answer is no, read this: [LINK]
